context_lines: Acts 14:23 should be read within the flow of its chapter and the larger argument of Acts.
common_interpretation_lines: There's also no process anywhere in Scripture for appointing a successor to "head of Jerusalem" the way there's an explicit process for appointing elders (Acts 14:23, Titus 1:5). If this were an office, we'd expect the text to show the office continuing past James. It doesn't. What we see instead looks like recognized personal standing — likely tied to James being a resurrection witness (1 Corinthians 15:7) and the Lord's own brother — operating within a plural eldership that never disappears around him.

That distinction matters, and it's an old and good one: respect for someone's maturity, wisdom, and demonstrated knowledge is not the same thing as installing them into a title that becomes an office others are formally appointed into. Every culture recognizes that some elders carry more weight than others because of experience or character. Scripture doesn't erase that. What it never does is turn James's personal standing into a title, a rank, or a hierarchy replicated in every church he never even visited.
